channel.title: NPCs on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in NPCs on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: NPC Reaction Pros And Cons
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction pros and cons.

This comes from a question from badim pakkin, who said: Tim, I was so hoping you could talk about the broader philosophy behind implementing or not implementing NPC triggers, SL reactions. I think that would be even more interesting than just specific implementation examples. He was asking that on a video I did on NPC’s triggering each other and I was giving a lot of examples, and this is certainly something I can talk about and I will. But funnily enough, if I don’t give a lot of examples in these videos, I frequently get comments from people saying: could you give me examples of that could you talk about in your game or in another game where you see something like the mechanic you’re talking about? So that’s why I give a lot of examples, but this is different. This is going to be the philosophy of why you would do it.

item.title: Event Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Event Systems.

These are some that I mentioned a while back in a video on NPC reactions, which I’ll link below, and last Friday I even mentioned NPCs triggering off each other, which talks about Event Systems as well, while the first video LED Lemmings 19 to ask: would you be interested in doing a video focused on events, how they’re triggered, how they’re handled, how they work, exist in single player versus multiplayer games like wild star, caveats, issues, problems, technical or otherwise, and whatever else you think is worth talking about? Sure, this is going to be probably aimed at programmers. I’m not going to give you implementation details, but any programmer who’s had more than one data structures class will know what I’m talking about when I’m saying: hey, you need to make a list of events. Maybe you want an array, maybe you want a link list, maybe you want a doubly link list, maybe you want a stack and you probably don’t want to. Stack anyway. You might want to Hash anyway.

item.title: NPCs Triggering Each Other
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC’s triggering each other.

This is in reaction to well a commenter, odesia hh2 TD, saw my NPC reaction video, which I’ll link below, and asked any ideas on how to expand this to make NPC’s trigger reactions between themselves and thus create emergent Behavior. I left a short comment and they replied. I guess the Magic in this case would be picking good triggers that facilitate the emergence of fun events and interactions. So an idea for a fun Friday: brainstorming cool triggers and interaction ideas. That’s this fun Friday, so let’s do it.

item.title: NPC Reactions
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction.

This comes from a question from AliceLittle1865, who asked: can you make a video, maybe on the topic of NPCs and companions? What I mean is how to create character reactions when they interact with the world or not, for example, how all the companions in New Vegas have some kind of reaction when on a mission or part of the map or player choice or faction. The same could be applied to a tabletop RPG. When the DM creates a character that the players have in their party or not, how would that character react to the interactions and development of the story and how do you prepare for that? I want to break this into two parts because the, at least for a video game, the complicated thing is how to set it up so you can do the reaction, and then I’ll talk very briefly on what things you might want to react to. Also, I call this NPC reaction. It is: commonly used for companion reaction, since companions are with you, are following you around and they see a lot of what you do. But I called this NPC reaction because you can really use it for anything in any character in the game who’s not the player. We’ve all played games where the NPCs react to things you do and say: my games all do that. To me that’s one of the just wonderful beautiful things about an RPG is reacting to what the player does and a big chunk of that reaction comes from having non-player characters say: I saw what you did, I saw what you said.

item.title: Believable Reactivity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about believable reactivity and, in other words, how do you make NPCs react to player choices in a way that makes sense in the RPG that you’re making?

This was a good question from Chaser 7, 2886, who asked: I was wondering how do you make choices in RPGs believable? What I mean is how do NPCs know you made the choice you made without making it like NPC’s magically know you made whatever choice you made? Okay, first of all, this is a good question, and I know I say that a lot, but this is a good question for two different reasons. One, thank you, Chaser 7, for actually clarifying your question. When you just said how do you make choices in RPGs believable, I thought you were going to say how do you make the NPC asking the quest to give to the player, how do you make that sound like a believable ask, like would the king actually ask me to go find his missing?

item.title: Writing Good NPCs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing good NPCs.

Now I’m going to start with the big caveat that I’ve said on here many times: I am not a good writer. I’ve tried to be good, I’ve worked on It’s something I can improve past a certain limited amount of. I can write lore text. I can write short narration, but personal one-on-one dialogue — no, I’m not that good at, but I am good at seeing good writing of that category.
